skill.name: openclaw-secure-linux-cloud
skill.description: Use when self-hosting OpenClaw on a cloud server, hardening a remote OpenClaw gateway, choosing between SSH tunneling, Tailscale, or reverse-proxy exposure, or reviewing Podman, pairing, sandboxing, token auth, and tool-permission defaults for a secure personal deployment.
skill.summary: Secure OpenClaw deployment guidance for Linux cloud servers with conservative hardening practices
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: All 116 static findings are false positives. The shell commands in REFERENCE.md are legitimate system administration examples for deploying and hardening OpenClaw on Linux servers. The sudo commands, firewall rules, and service management operations are standard security practices for server hardening. This skill provides defensive security guidance without any malicious functionality or data exfiltration patterns.

content.value_statement: Self-hosting OpenClaw on public cloud servers requires careful security hardening to prevent unauthorized access. This skill provides a conservative, production-ready deployment pattern with SSH tunneling, loopback binding, and defense-in-depth practices specifically designed for Linux cloud environments.
actual_capabilities: Generate cross-distro Linux hardening commands for OpenClaw servers
Compare SSH tunneling vs Tailscale vs reverse proxy access methods
Provide secure Podman-based OpenClaw deployment configurations
Create pre-launch security checklists for cloud deployments
Audit existing OpenClaw installations for security misconfigurations
Guide token authentication and permission hardening best practices
limitations: Linux-focused guidance with Debian/Ubuntu examples, requires adaptation for other distributions
Assumes rootless Podman container runtime, not applicable to all deployment environments
Conservative security posture may be more restrictive than casual development setups require
Does not cover Docker Compose or non-Linux platform deployment patterns

use_cases.title: Secure Cloud Server Setup
use_cases.description: Deploy OpenClaw on a public cloud VM with proper firewall rules, SSH hardening, and private gateway access. User needs a production-ready security posture from day one.
use_cases.target_user: DevOps engineer deploying OpenClaw to production infrastructure

use_cases.title: Security Audit
use_cases.description: Review an existing OpenClaw deployment for common misconfigurations like public port exposure, overly permissive tool access, or weak authentication settings.
use_cases.target_user: System administrator hardening a running OpenClaw instance

use_cases.title: Remote Access Planning
use_cases.description: Choose between SSH tunneling, Tailscale, or reverse proxy for remote OpenClaw Control UI access based on user convenience requirements and security constraints.
use_cases.target_user: Developer deciding how to access OpenClaw gateway remotely

prompt_templates.title: Basic Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me deploy OpenClaw securely on a Debian cloud server. I want to access the Control UI from my laptop without exposing the gateway to the public internet.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time OpenClaw setup on cloud infrastructure

prompt_templates.title: Security Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my OpenClaw deployment for security issues. The server is at [IP address] and I'm currently accessing the Control UI directly over HTTP.

prompt_templates.title: Access Method Selection
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to access my cloud-hosted OpenClaw instance from multiple devices. Should I use SSH tunneling, Tailscale, or set up a reverse proxy with authentication?
prompt_templates.scenario: Choosing remote access strategy for multi-device usage

prompt_templates.title: Configuration Hardening
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a secure OpenClaw configuration for a cloud server. Lock down tool permissions, enable sandboxing, and ensure the gateway only accepts loopback connections.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating production-ready OpenClaw configuration with strict security defaults

output_examples.input: Help me deploy OpenClaw securely on Ubuntu 22.04.
output_examples.output: I'll help you deploy OpenClaw securely on Ubuntu 22.04. This will involve: 1) Hardening the host with SSH keys and firewall rules, 2) Installing rootless Podman, 3) Deploying OpenClaw on loopback only, 4) Accessing the Control UI through an SSH tunnel. First, let's set up SSH key authentication from your local machine...

To audit your deployment, I need to check several key areas: 1) Gateway binding (should be loopback, not 0.0.0.0), 2) Firewall rules (port 18789 should not be public), 3) Authentication mode (token auth enabled), 4) File permissions (~/.openclaw should be 700). Please run these commands on your server so I can review the current configuration...

output_examples.input: Should I use Tailscale or SSH tunneling?
output_examples.output: For your first deployment, I recommend SSH tunneling as the default approach. It provides the smallest attack surface and is easiest to verify. Tailscale becomes advantageous when you need frequent access from multiple trusted devices without maintaining active SSH sessions. Both approaches keep the gateway private, which is the critical security goal.

best_practices: Always bind the OpenClaw gateway to loopback (127.0.0.1) rather than exposing it directly on public network interfaces
Use SSH tunneling for initial remote access, then upgrade to Tailscale or reverse proxy only when legitimate use cases emerge
Keep the OpenClaw config directory (~/.openclaw) locked down with chmod 700 and config files at chmod 600
anti_patterns: Never bind the gateway to 0.0.0.0 or expose port 18789 through the firewall just because authentication is enabled
Do not disable token authentication or sandboxing purely for convenience during initial setup
Avoid granting broad tool permissions (runtime, filesystem, automation, browser access) before identifying actual workflow requirements

faq.question: Can I use this skill for local development setups?
faq.answer: This skill focuses on cloud server hardening. For local single-machine setups where remote access is not needed, the official OpenClaw onboarding docs may be more appropriate.

faq.question: Does this work with Docker instead of Podman?
faq.answer: The reference documentation uses rootless Podman. Docker requires different security considerations. Adapt the hardening principles, but verify specific container runtime guidance for Docker deployments.

faq.question: Is reverse proxy access ever safe to use?
faq.answer: Reverse proxy access is appropriate only when you explicitly need public exposure and accept the additional hardening burden. Private-first approaches (SSH tunnel, Tailscale) remain preferred when they meet your needs.

faq.question: What Linux distributions are supported?
faq.answer: The examples are Debian/Ubuntu specific, but the security model generalizes. For Fedora, RHEL, Arch, or other distros, the principles apply but package managers and firewall commands differ.

faq.question: Can I skip SSH tunneling if I use strong authentication?
faq.answer: Network boundaries provide defense-in-depth that authentication alone cannot replace. Even with strong auth, keeping the gateway private reduces attack surface. Use SSH tunneling or Tailscale unless you have a clear requirement for broader access.

faq.question: How often should I run security audits?
faq.answer: Run audits after any configuration change, permission adjustment, or sandbox policy update. The reference doc provides commands for both quick security checks and deep audits.
